skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation skill for inference.sh that opens pages, interacts with elements, captures screenshots, records sessions, and runs page JavaScript.
skill.summary: Automate browser sessions with element refs, screenshots, video, proxies, uploads, and page scripting.
skill.author: tul-sh

security_audit.summary: The static findings mostly reflect real browser automation capabilities rather than hidden malware. However, the skill documents credentialed sessions, cookie extraction, arbitrary page JavaScript, proxy routing, screenshots, video recording, file upload, and shell templates, so it should not publish without stronger safeguards and warnings. No evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

high_findings.title: Cookie Extraction From Authenticated Sessions
high_findings.description: The authentication guide shows extracting document.cookie from a logged-in browser session and printing the result. This can expose session tokens or account cookies if used on sensitive sites.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents cookie extraction from an authenticated session. The surrounding section frames this as a supported pattern, not an accidental scanner match.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: The skill supports an execute function that runs caller-provided JavaScript in the active page context. On authenticated pages, this can collect page data, cookies exposed to scripts, or internal content.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execute function is documented as a core capability with free-form code input. The risk is contextual but direct and highly capable.

high_findings.title: Credentialed Browser Automation
high_findings.description: The templates and authentication guide automate login flows with environment-provided usernames and passwords. This is legitimate but creates high impact if logs, recordings, or extracted output leak secrets.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential handling is explicitly documented and implemented in templates. The guidance includes some safeguards, but the capability remains sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots and Video Can Capture Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The skill captures screenshots and video of browser sessions, including typed input and authenticated pages. The video guide warns against sensitive sessions, but the capture feature is central.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Screen and video capture are documented capabilities. The documentation includes a warning, which lowers confidence that this is unsafe by default.

medium_findings.title: Proxy Routing Including SOCKS Proxies
medium_findings.description: The proxy guide documents corporate proxies, credentialed proxies, and SOCKS5 routing. This can be useful for testing but can also hide traffic origin or bypass network policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Proxy configuration is explicitly supported. The examples are legitimate, but SOCKS routing and proxy credentials create meaningful misuse potential.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill can upload local file paths into browser file inputs. This is useful for testing but may disclose local files if an agent receives an unsafe path or target.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File upload is listed as a supported interaction and accepts file paths. No hidden upload destination is present, so the risk depends on user authorization.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Findings Are Mostly Intended Examples
low_findings.description: Most command-substitution alerts are shell snippets that invoke infsh, jq, or template variables. They are not hidden execution paths, but users should review shell templates before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are real shell code, but the commands are visible examples and templates. No evidence found of covert command execution beyond the documented tool usage.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts are examples such as example.com or documentation links. They do not identify a hidden exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are in markdown examples or public documentation references. No suspicious external collection endpoint was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Markdown Reference Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts point to relative documentation links such as parent-directory references. No evidence found of filesystem traversal logic in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited paths are documentation links, not code that reads arbitrary files. The confidence is high enough to dismiss this specific static pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Access to infsh Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access to infsh commands and provides shell templates. This is expected for the skill, but it expands execution risk compared with pure documentation skills.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and executable templates are direct evidence. The risk is controlled to infsh but still operationally meaningful.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Capture Outputs
dangerous_patterns.description: Browser outputs include screenshots, videos, element text, page text, and extracted links. These artifacts can include private data from authenticated or internal pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files document output artifacts that persist page content. The risk is clear when targets contain private data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie and Credential Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documentation covers credentials, session reuse, and cookie extraction. These patterns need explicit user consent and strong redaction rules.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive patterns are explicit and line-local. The documentation warns against logging passwords, but cookie extraction remains documented.

content.value_statement: Manual browser tasks are slow to repeat and hard to verify. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through scripted web navigation, capture, and testing workflows.
actual_capabilities: Open web pages through the inference.sh agent-browser command.
Interact with page elements using returned @e references.
Capture viewport or full-page screenshots from active sessions.
Record browser sessions and return video when recording is enabled.
Run JavaScript in the current page context for extraction or inspection.
Use templates for form automation, authenticated sessions, and content capture.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and access to the hosted agent-browser app.
Element references expire after navigation or dynamic page changes.
Authenticated workflows require user-provided credentials and careful handling.
Screenshots, videos, cookies, and extracted text may contain sensitive data.

use_cases.title: Automated QA Checks
use_cases.description: Run repeatable browser flows, capture screenshots, and record failures for review.

use_cases.title: Research Capture
use_cases.description: Collect visible page text, links, screenshots, and page metadata from public websites.

use_cases.title: Operational Form Tasks
use_cases.description: Fill known forms, verify submission results, and keep a session record for internal workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to open this URL, list the visible interactive elements, and summarize the page state.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Public Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this form, identify the field refs, fill the provided non-sensitive test data, submit it, and verify the result.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Evidence for a Bug
prompt_templates.prompt: Reproduce this browser issue, take screenshots before and after the key action, and record the session if no secrets are visible.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Structured Page Content
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target page, snapshot current refs, run safe JavaScript to extract headings and links, and save a screenshot for context.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and summarize the browser state.
output_examples.output: The page loaded successfully. The report includes the title, current URL, visible buttons, form fields, and a screenshot reference.

output_examples.input: Run a form submission test with sample data.
output_examples.output: The workflow identified the fields, filled the sample values, clicked submit, and reported whether a success message appeared.

output_examples.input: Record a navigation bug without credentials.
output_examples.output: The session produced a short recording, a final screenshot, and notes about the failed click target.

best_practices: Use only test accounts or approved credentials for authenticated browsing.
Disable recording when pages may show passwords, tokens, personal data, or private customer content.
Re-run snapshot after every navigation before using element references.
anti_patterns: Do not extract or print cookies unless the user explicitly authorizes that action.
Do not route traffic through proxies to bypass access controls or site policies.
Do not upload local files unless the file path and destination are both approved.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interaction, screenshots, video recording, uploads, and page-level JavaScript through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it need an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on the inference.sh CLI and the hosted agent-browser app.

faq.question: Can it handle login pages?
faq.answer: Yes, but users must provide approved credentials and avoid recording or logging sensitive fields.

faq.question: Can it scrape private pages?
faq.answer: It can extract content visible to the browser session. Users must have permission for the target page.

faq.question: Why are screenshots and video risky?
faq.answer: They may capture credentials, tokens, personal data, internal pages, or customer information.

faq.question: Should this skill run on untrusted sites?
faq.answer: Use caution. Untrusted pages can influence visible content, navigation, and extracted data.
